CLASP improves low-resource semantic parsing performance of moderate-sized models.
CLASP is evaluated on the English PIZZA dataset with as few as 16 real examples, achieving performance gains over strong baselines.
CLASP is evaluated on the mTOP cross-lingual zero-shot setting, enabling a model trained only on English to generalize to four new languages with performance gains over strong baselines.
Augmentation methods for various misspelling types are proposed to train neural spell-checking models for endangered languages.
An evaluation resource for four Peruvian indigenous languages (Shipibo-Konibo, Asháninka, Yánesha, Yine) is created.
An ensemble model trained with augmented data from various error types achieves better performance across most error types and languages.
Spell-checkers are released as a web service for indigenous communities and organisations to develop language materials.
CoFE provides a dataset of 4.2k proposals and 20k comments from an online European participatory democracy platform, featuring intra-multilingual pairs in over 40% of cases.
CoFE includes stance annotations for more than 7k comment/proposal pairs in 26 languages via self-tagging, and manually annotated 1,206 comments in six languages with a Krippendorff’s α of 0.69.
CoFE defines an intra-multilingual multi-target stance classification task for online debates.
COVIDNEWS-NER is a COVID-19 news NER dataset containing 3,000 hand‑annotated strongly labeled sentences and 13,000 auto‑generated weakly labeled sentences.
CONTROSTER is a recipe that strategically combines weak and strong labels to improve NER for emerging topics through transfer learning.
Initializing a model with weakly labeled data before fine‑tuning on strongly labeled data outperforms training on only strong or only weak data.
Combining out‑of‑domain and in‑domain weak label training prevents performance saturation that occurs when training on weak labels from a single source.
Role-Aware Centrality (RAC) model captures interactions among different roles in dialogue summarization.
RAC assigns each role a sentence-level centrality score using role prompts.
RAC measures both utterance importance and relevance between roles and utterances.
RAC re-weights context representations, which are used by the decoder to generate role-specific summaries.
RAC can be integrated with any sequence-to-sequence model.
RAC achieves state-of-the-art performance on the CSDS and MC benchmark datasets.
Analyses demonstrate that role-aware centrality improves the precision of generated role summaries.
Ext-PIE-Net is a self-supervised pre-training method that leverages off-the-shelf multi-modal hate-speech data and multiple specialized pretext tasks for meme analysis.
MM-SimCLR is a self-supervised pre-training method that leverages off-the-shelf multi-modal hate-speech data and multiple specialized pretext tasks for meme analysis.
Ext-PIE-Net and MM-SimCLR achieve performance competitive with fully supervised baselines under label-efficient training on the Hateful Memes task.
Ext-PIE-Net and MM-SimCLR outperform fully supervised baselines on all three tasks of the Memotion challenge.
Ext-PIE-Net and MM-SimCLR achieve competitive performance on the HarMeme task, demonstrating generalizability across meme analysis datasets.
Representations learned by Ext-PIE-Net and MM-SimCLR enable effective task-specific learning with fewer labeled training samples.
The complexity of the self-supervision strategy correlates with downstream task performance in meme analysis.
C3PO translates pseudocode to programming language code using a lightweight copying mechanism that reduces reliance on large language models.
The copy phase of C3PO employs a binary classifier to identify and mask pseudocode tokens that can be directly copied into the target code.
The generate phase of C3PO uses a sequence-to-sequence model to produce code for the masked positions.
The combine phase of C3PO merges the generated code with the copied tokens to form the final program.
C3PO achieves comparable performance to models without the copying mechanism while lowering training computational cost and reducing vocabulary size.
A metric that quantifies self-repetition in neural summarizer outputs as the count of n-grams of length four or longer that appear in multiple outputs of the same system.
An empirical regression analysis shows that BART has a higher propensity for self-repetition than T5 and Pegasus when fine-tuned on five datasets.
Fine-tuning on more abstractive data and on data containing formulaic language is linked to higher self-repetition rates in neural summarizers.
Qualitative analysis identifies that neural summarizers generate artefacts such as advertisements, disclaimers, and domain-specific formulaic phrases unrelated to the source content.
A corpus-level analysis approach for self-repetition is introduced to help practitioners clean training data and to support methods for minimizing self-repetition in summarization systems.
MUTE is a multimodal dataset of 4158 memes with Bengali and code-mixed captions for hateful meme detection.
Detailed annotation guidelines are provided to aid creation of similar datasets in other resource-constrained languages.
Extensive experiments on MUTE evaluate visual-only, textual-only, and multimodal models for hateful meme classification.
Joint evaluation of visual and textual features improves hateful meme classification compared to unimodal evaluation.
Hengam is an adversarially trained transformer for Persian temporal tagging.
HengamTagger is an extensible rule-based tool that extracts temporal expressions from language-specific patterns for any language.
HengamTagger was applied to a large Persian text collection to produce weakly labeled temporal tagging data (HengamCorpus).
An adversarially trained transformer model was introduced that generalizes over HengamTagger’s rules.
HengamGold is the first high-quality gold‑standard dataset for Persian temporal tagging.
The adversarial HengamTransformer achieves the best performance among Persian temporal tagging methods.
The adversarial HengamTransformer successfully handles language ambiguities and incorrect spellings.
Sixteen bias probing analyses were conducted on four Norwegian and four English pre-trained language models using simple bias probes.
Adding negation to bias probes does not substantially change the correlation between gendered pronouns and occupations in the evaluated language models.
Altering the grammatical tense of verbs in bias probes affects model behavior and the observed gender‑occupation correlations.
Aggregating bias probe results across grammatical tenses yields a more representative measurement of gender‑occupation correlations.
